right.reveal: You sue for the true value of the lost gas and the scorched land. The investor climate cools and the case drags, but you set a precedent that negligence here has a price.

speaker: Energy Secretary
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. Gas is running out, so you stopped giving gas pipes to new homes. Millions of angry people are waiting.

left.label: Give gas to the homes to make people happy.
left.tooltip: A promised connection denied is a vote lost.
left.reveal: The taps open for the waiting millions and the goodwill flows. The reserve drains faster toward the day the gas simply runs out, for homes and factories alike.

right.label: Keep the gas locked up and save it for factories.
right.tooltip: Gas burned in a stove builds nothing; gas in a factory builds a country.
right.reveal: You keep the freeze and steer the gas to industry and power. The waiting millions seethe, but the reserve stretches further and the factories keep running.

speaker: Bridges Authority Chairman
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. A cool new river tunnel was built with borrowed money, but hardly anyone drives through it.

left.label: Charge a high price to drive through so you can pay back the loan.
left.tooltip: The users of the tunnel should pay for it.
left.reveal: The tolls bite and the debt is serviced. Hauliers reroute to avoid the cost, traffic thins further, and the showpiece becomes a monument to optimistic forecasts.

right.label: Keep the price low and let the government lose money.
right.tooltip: A tunnel no one can afford to use is a tomb.
right.reveal: You keep the toll cheap and the traffic builds. The debt sits unpaid on the public ledger, a quiet weight that some future budget will have to carry.

speaker: Transport Electrification Adviser
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. Buying gas from other countries costs too much money. You can help people buy electric cars instead, but it will upset the gas stations and use a lot of electricity.

left.label: Push hard to get everyone electric cars.
left.tooltip: Every electric mile is a dollar that stays home.
left.reveal: Incentives and chargers roll out and the EVs come. The fuel-import bill begins its long decline, while the grid strains and the petrol-pump economy starts to wither.

right.label: Keep using regular gas cars.
right.tooltip: A grid this fragile cannot charge a nation's cars.
right.reveal: You hold off and keep the petrol economy intact. The fuel-import bill keeps bleeding dollars, but the strained grid is spared and the pump-workers keep their trade.

speaker: Renewable Energy Adviser
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. A foreign company wants to build giant windmills on the beach for clean power. But this will take up the land where fishermen and farmers work.

left.label: Say yes and build the windmills on the beach.
left.tooltip: Wind is the one fuel no one can embargo.
left.reveal: The turbines rise and clean power flows. The fishers and salt-farmers lose ground and the tariff is steep, but the country gains a fuel free of import and emission.

right.label: Say no to protect the fishermen and farmers.
right.tooltip: A green plant on stolen land is not clean.
right.reveal: You refuse to displace the coastal livelihoods for the turbines. The clean-power gain is forgone, but the fishers and salt-farmers keep the shore they have always worked.

speaker: Land Reform Commissioner
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. New mud islands just popped up in the river, and they belong to the government.

left.label: Let rich bullies steal the islands.
left.tooltip: The chars are already governed by those who hold them.
left.reveal: The mastans get their titles and the landless become their tenants. A vast tract of fertile new land entrenches the very feudalism land reform was meant to end.

right.label: Give the islands to poor, homeless farmers.
right.tooltip: New land should make new owners, not new landlords.
right.reveal: You title the chars to the families who farm them. The strongmen lose a prize and send their thugs, but thousands of landless households gain ground of their own.

speaker: Wetlands Director
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. Farmers are building walls in the big wetlands to grow more food. But the walls destroy nature and hurt the birds and fish.

left.label: Let them build walls to grow more food.
left.tooltip: More crops from the same water is progress.
left.reveal: The dikes go up and the rice doubles for a while. The fishery crashes, the birds vanish, and one bad flash-flood now drowns a crop the wetland used to absorb.

right.label: Stop them to protect nature and the animals.
right.tooltip: A wetland drained is a famine deferred.
right.reveal: You halt the conversion and protect the flood-pulse. The single-crop yields stay modest, but the fishery, the birds, and the wetland's flood defence all survive.

speaker: Tourism Board Chair
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. Your beautiful coral island is being ruined by too many tourists and their trash.

left.label: Let everyone keep coming for the money.
left.tooltip: The island earns its keep in tourist taka.
left.reveal: The hotels stay and the boats keep coming. The reef bleaches and breaks under the crowds, and the one coral ecosystem in the country slides toward death.

right.label: Stop some people from visiting and tear down bad hotels.
right.tooltip: A dead reef draws no one.
right.reveal: You ration access and raze the illegal hotels. The local tourist economy takes a blow and the resort-owners sue, but the reef gets a fighting chance to recover.

speaker: Environment Secretary
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. Plastic trash is clogging up all the rivers and making the city flood. A helper wants to ban plastic bags completely, but plastic makers will be very angry.

left.label: Don't ban the plastic bags yet.
left.tooltip: A ban that kills an industry kills jobs and donors.
left.reveal: You spare the plastics industry and the bags keep flowing. The drains clog, the floods worsen, and the microplastics work their way into the fish and the people.

right.label: Ban the plastic bags and force people to use safe cloth bags.
right.tooltip: A drain choked with plastic drowns a city.
right.reveal: You ban the worst single-use plastics and revive the jute-bag trade. The plastics lobby rages and some jobs vanish, but the drains clear and a jute revival stirs.

speaker: Geological Survey Director
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. The beaches have special sand that a company wants to dig up for money. But digging it up will destroy the beach and the turtle nests.

left.label: Let them dig up the beach for money.
left.tooltip: Rare earths in the sand are riches wasted unmined.
left.reveal: The miners move in and the export earnings begin. The dunes that blunt the cyclones are carried away by the truckload, and the coast loses its natural armour.

right.label: Say no and protect the beach and turtles.
right.tooltip: Dunes that stop a cyclone are worth more than zircon.
right.reveal: You refuse the concession. The mineral wealth stays buried, but the dunes keep standing between the storms and the people behind them.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. The walls holding the ocean back are old and breaking. A big storm could drown everyone.

left.label: Just patch the broken spots for now.
left.tooltip: A full rebuild is a budget I will spend on the next disaster.
left.reveal: You fund only the emergency repairs. Each cyclone breaches the weak embankments anew, salting fields and drowning homes the rebuild would have saved.

right.label: Spend a lot of money to completely rebuild the walls.
right.tooltip: An embankment is cheap next to the district it saves.
right.reveal: You commit to rebuilding the polders properly. The cost is huge and slow to show, but a coastal district gains a real wall against the rising, angrier sea.

speaker: Industries Secretary
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. Builders are buying rocks from other countries. You can blast your own hills to get rocks instead, but those hills are sacred to native people.

left.label: Blast the sacred hills for rocks.
left.tooltip: A boom this big cannot run on imports forever.
left.reveal: The quarry opens and the cement-import bill eases. The sacred range is blasted apart, the indigenous communities are displaced, and another grievance hardens in the hills.

right.label: Keep buying rocks from other countries.
right.tooltip: Some hills are worth more standing than crushed.
right.reveal: You leave the range intact and keep paying for imports. The dollar drain continues, but the forest and the communities who revere it are spared the dynamite.

speaker: Fisheries Director
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. Fish farmers are giving their fish illegal medicine. Other countries won't buy the fish if they find out.

left.label: Pretend you didn't notice and hope no one finds out.
left.tooltip: A crackdown now empties a thousand ponds.
left.reveal: You keep the exports flowing on a wink. One bad batch detected abroad triggers a blanket ban, and a whole export sector collapses overnight on a gamble.

right.label: Stop the farmers from using the illegal medicine.
right.tooltip: An export caught dirty loses the whole market.
right.reveal: You enforce the residue limits hard. The farmers howl at the cost and the disease losses, but the exports clear European labs and the market stays open.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. The rivers are full of mud and big boats are getting stuck. You can dig the mud out fast and dump it anywhere, or do it carefully so it doesn't hurt the fish.

left.label: Dig fast and dump the mud anywhere.
left.tooltip: Open rivers move a nation; tidy disposal can wait.
left.reveal: The channels reopen and the cargo flows again. The carelessly dumped spoil buries floodplains and chokes fisheries, trading one waterway's life for another's.

right.label: Dig carefully so you don't hurt the fish.
right.tooltip: A river reopened by ruining another is no gain.
right.reveal: You dredge with proper spoil management and floodplain protection. It is slower and dearer, but the rivers reopen without killing the wetlands beside them.

speaker: Petrobangla Chairman
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. Big companies want to dig for gas deep in your ocean. They want an easy deal where they keep most of the money.

left.label: Give them an easy deal so they start working fast.
left.tooltip: Gas undiscovered earns nothing; bring the rigs.
left.reveal: The sweet terms draw the majors and the drilling begins. Gas may flow within years, but the low royalties mean the nation pockets a thin slice of its own seabed.

right.label: Demand a fair share for your country.
right.tooltip: Our seabed, our terms.
right.reveal: You hold out for higher royalties and faster work commitments. Some bidders walk and exploration slows, but any gas found will pay the nation what it is worth.

speaker: Commerce Secretary
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. The price of salt is too low, and your poor salt farmers are going broke. Other countries sell it even cheaper.

left.label: Let the cheap salt in from other countries.
left.tooltip: Industry runs on the cheapest input.
left.reveal: The factories get their cheap salt and the lobby is pleased. The coastal salt-farmers, undercut to ruin, abandon the pans, and a coastal livelihood dies out.

right.label: Ban the cheap salt to protect your poor farmers.
right.tooltip: A coast of ruined salt-pans is a coast of empty hands.
right.reveal: You shut out the foreign salt and prop up the local price. Industry grumbles at the cost, but the salt-farmers keep their pans and their precarious living.

speaker: Forest Conservator
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. You need to plant more trees. You can plant cheap, fast-growing trees that hurt the soil, or slow, natural trees that help animals.

left.label: Plant the cheap, fast trees.
left.tooltip: A number on a chart is a number won.
left.reveal: The hills go green with acacia and the coverage statistics soar. Beneath the uniform canopy nothing lives, and the water table drops under the thirsty exotics.

right.label: Plant the slow, natural trees.
right.tooltip: A plantation is not a forest.
right.reveal: You fund mixed native restoration. The 'green coverage' rises slowly and the targets slip, but a real forest — water, wildlife, and all — begins to return.

speaker: Petrobangla Chairman
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. You found a lot of extra gas under the ground. Use it all up fast so things are cheap right now, or save it for the future?

left.label: Use it up fast to make things cheap now.
left.tooltip: Plenty now buys loyalty now.
left.reveal: You open the taps and the country enjoys cheap, abundant gas. The field empties in a handful of years, and the windfall is gone before it built anything lasting.

right.label: Save it carefully for the future.
right.tooltip: A field burned in five years is five years of folly.
right.reveal: You meter the new field for industry and the long haul. The public sees no instant bonanza, but the reserve underwrites a decade of factories and fertiliser.

speaker: Power Development Chairman
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. The dirty power plant next to the beautiful forest wants to build a second, bigger plant right next to it. Scientists say this is a terrible idea.

left.label: Let them build the second dirty power plant.
left.tooltip: Proven ground builds the cheapest megawatts.
left.reveal: The expansion is greenlit and the capacity doubles. So does the ash, the warm effluent, and the shipping traffic gnawing at the forest's edge.

right.label: Say no and build clean power somewhere else.
right.tooltip: I will not double a bet against the forest.
right.reveal: You forbid the second unit and steer new capacity to cleaner sites. The cheap megawatts are forgone, but the mangrove shield is not made to carry twice the load.

speaker: Hill Resources Officer
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. You found valuable rocks under the land where native people live in the hills. They say you must ask permission and share the money.

left.label: Take the rocks without asking permission.
left.tooltip: The nation's minerals are the nation's, not a clan's.
left.reveal: You authorise extraction over the communities' objections. The minerals flow and so does the fury; the peace accord, already brittle, cracks a little wider.

right.label: Ask permission and share the money with them.
right.tooltip: Wealth dug from a people's land owes that people.
right.reveal: You make extraction conditional on consent and royalties to the communities. The lobby fumes at the precedent, but the hills, for once, share in their own ground's wealth.

speaker: Irrigation Authority Head
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. Farmers pump water for free, but they waste a lot. You can make them pay for it using prepaid cards, but they might go broke if it's a bad year.

left.label: Make them use prepaid cards to pay for water.
left.tooltip: Water and power given free are water and power wasted.
left.reveal: The meters go on and the reckless pumping eases, sparing the aquifer and the subsidy. In the dry years, though, a metered pump becomes one more cost a failing farmer cannot meet.

right.label: Let them keep pumping water for free.
right.tooltip: A meter on a farmer's pump is a meter on his harvest.
right.reveal: You leave the pumps free and the farmers easy. The aquifer keeps falling and the subsidy keeps bleeding, but no farmer loses a crop to a card with no credit on it.

speaker: Labour Inspector
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. The places where men take apart old ships are deadly. People get hurt or die. Bosses say making it safe costs too much money.

left.label: Leave the deadly shipyards alone.
left.tooltip: Cheap steel needs cheap hands.
left.reveal: The yards keep running on blood and asbestos. The steel stays cheap, the casualty list grows, and the next exposé brands the beaches the world's most lethal workplace.

right.label: Force the bosses to make the shipyards safe.
right.tooltip: Steel bought with a man a week is too dear.
right.reveal: You force protective gear, asbestos controls, and an end to child labour. Margins thin and some yards close, but men stop dying by the dozen on the tide line.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. Engineers want to dig a giant ditch to move water from wet places to dry places. It costs a fortune and might ruin the rivers.

left.label: Say yes to digging the giant water ditch.
left.tooltip: A nation that moves its own water masters its fate.
left.reveal: The mega-project launches to fanfare and a flood of contracts. The drought-prone north dreams of water, while ecologists count the rivers the canal will starve and drown.

right.label: Say no and stick to smaller, safer ideas.
right.tooltip: A canal that breaks two rivers to fill one is folly.
right.reveal: You set aside the grand scheme for modest local water projects. The headline-grabbing fix is gone, but the rivers keep their flow and the money spreads wider.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. The giant ditch is taking too much water. The wet places are now drying out, and angry people want you to turn the water down.

left.label: Keep taking all the water to the dry places.
left.tooltip: The north's thirst was the whole point.
left.reveal: You hold the flow and the north blooms. The donor basin's fisheries collapse and its people, betrayed, turn the canal into a symbol of one region drained for another.

right.label: Turn the water down to save the wet places.
right.tooltip: I will not drown one region to water another.
right.reveal: You cut the diversion back to a sustainable flow. The north's gains shrink and its boosters grumble, but the donor basin's wetlands and fishers are pulled back from ruin.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. The builders of the ditch are mad because you slowed them down. They threaten to quit unless you pay them all the money anyway.

left.label: Pay the builders all the money.
left.tooltip: A half-dug canal is worse than none.
left.reveal: You pay out to keep the works moving. The connected contractors pocket the windfall, the budget bleeds, and the project limps on under a cloud of inflated bills.

right.label: Tell them to follow the rules and only pay for what they do.
right.tooltip: I will not be extorted by my own diggers.
right.reveal: You refuse the padded claim and renegotiate to the reduced scope. The contractors threaten and litigate, but the public is spared paying full price for half a canal.

speaker: Grid Operations Chief
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. The country's power wires are so weak that one mistake could turn off the lights for everyone at once. Fixing it costs a ton of money.

left.label: Spend the money to fix the wires perfectly.
left.tooltip: A grid that fails all at once fails the whole nation at once.
left.reveal: You fund islanding, redundancy, and modern controls. The cost is heavy and the benefit invisible between failures, but a single fault can no longer plunge the entire country into darkness.

right.label: Just put tape on it and hope the lights stay on.
right.tooltip: A full redesign is a budget for a disaster that may not come.
right.reveal: You make do with patches. The money goes elsewhere, the brittle grid stays brittle, and the country waits, one bad fault away from another total nationwide blackout.

speaker: Rural Electrification Chief
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. Faraway villages don't have electricity. You can give them small solar panels now, or make them wait years for big power lines to be built.

left.label: Give them the small solar panels right now.
left.tooltip: Light tonight beats a grid in a decade.
left.reveal: You blanket the remote villages with solar home systems. Millions get clean light and phone-charging now, though the small panels cannot run the mills and pumps a grid eventually would.

right.label: Make them wait for the big power lines.
right.tooltip: A village deserves real power, not a panel.
right.reveal: You hold out to bring the villages the full grid. They wait years longer in darkness, but when the lines arrive they carry the heavy loads that solar panels never could.

speaker: Power Transition Adviser
prompt: It comes back sharpened by every season you survived. People want you to shut down old, dirty coal plants early to clean the air. But shutting them down will leave thousands of workers without jobs.

left.label: Shut down the dirty plants and try to help the workers.
left.tooltip: A dying industry should be eased to rest, not abandoned.
left.reveal: You retire the plants early and fund retraining and new industry for the coal towns. The stranded-asset cost is steep, but the air clears, the image brightens, and the workers are not simply discarded.

right.label: Keep the dirty plants open so workers keep their jobs.
right.tooltip: I will not strand billions and a workforce for an early exit.
right.reveal: You keep the coal plants running to their full life. The assets and jobs are preserved, the towns stay employed, and the dirty smoke and the climate criticism keep mounting year on year.

speaker: Energy Security Director
prompt: A later year of power, the old crisis now a true test of it. The country only has enough gas for a few days if there is an emergency. Building a giant tank to save extra gas costs a lot.

left.label: Build the giant tank to save extra gas for emergencies.
left.tooltip: A nation with days of fuel is a nation days from paralysis.
left.reveal: You build the strategic reserve and expand storage. The capital outlay is large, but the next supply shock or blockade meets a buffer instead of an instant fuel crisis.

right.label: Do not build the tank and hope there are no emergencies.
right.tooltip: A reserve is a fortune frozen against a maybe.
right.reveal: You keep stocks thin to save the capital. The money stays free for other needs, and the country stays days away from paralysis should any supply shock or blockade come.

speaker: Energy Access Director
prompt: The unresolved returns, and this time it will not be deferred. People in villages cook over smoky fires that hurt their lungs and chop down too many trees. You can pay to help them buy clean cooking gas.

left.label: Help pay for clean cooking gas.
left.tooltip: A woman's lungs are worth more than the price of a cylinder.
left.reveal: You subsidise LPG and build safe distribution. Woodsmoke recedes from kitchens, women's health improves and the forests get a reprieve, and the subsidy and cylinder-safety burden fall on the budget.

right.label: Let them keep cooking over smoky fires.
right.tooltip: An LPG subsidy is a cost the budget cannot carry.
right.reveal: You leave rural kitchens to firewood and dung. The budget is spared, and women keep breathing woodsmoke into ruined lungs while the forests keep falling for fuel.

speaker: Energy Diplomacy Director
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. You can share a big gas pipe with a neighbor for cheap energy. But if you get in a fight, they could turn off your gas.

left.label: Build the shared gas pipe.
left.tooltip: Steady gas for decades is worth a careful diplomacy.
left.reveal: You commit to the pipeline and cheaper gas begins to flow. Your energy security improves and your dependence deepens, your grid now hostage to a transit route and a neighbour's goodwill.

right.label: Don't do it, and find your own gas at home.
right.tooltip: A pipeline through a neighbour is a valve in his hand.
right.reveal: You forgo the pipeline for domestic exploration and LNG. The gas is costlier and slower, but no foreign hand rests on the valve that feeds your grid.

speaker: Atomic Energy Chairman
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. The nuclear plant will make dangerous glowing trash. You can spend money to build a safe hiding spot, or trust the builders who pinky-promised to take it back later.

left.label: Spend the money to build a safe hiding spot now.
left.tooltip: A poison that lasts ten thousand years deserves a plan.
left.reveal: You invest in genuine spent-fuel storage and safety. The cost is large and the benefit unseen for decades, but you do not leave a radioactive legacy to be improvised by your grandchildren.

right.label: Trust the builders to take it back later.
right.tooltip: The Russians said they would handle it; let them.
right.reveal: You lean on the foreign builder's vague assurances and defer the problem. The money is saved now, and a growing pile of spent fuel waits on a promise no contract firmly guarantees.

speaker: Power Regulator
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. Big factories are building their own private power plants because the city's power breaks too much. This makes the city's power even worse for normal people.

left.label: Force the factories to use the city's power.
left.tooltip: A grid the strong abandon collapses on the weak.
left.reveal: You compel the factories to draw from the grid and surrender their gas. The grid's finances and reliability improve for all, and the industrialists rage at losing their dependable captive power.

right.label: Let the factories use their own private power.
right.tooltip: A factory that cannot trust the grid will make its own.
right.reveal: You let the factories self-supply. Industry keeps its reliable power, and the grid, abandoned by its best customers, grows weaker and dearer for the households and small firms left on it.

speaker: Agriculture Research Director
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. Scientists made special seeds that bugs won't eat. It helps grow more food, but people are scared it's unnatural and gives too much power to the seed company.

left.label: Use the special seeds to grow more food.
left.tooltip: A crop that needs no poison feeds more for less.
left.reveal: You approve the GM variety and yields rise as pesticide use falls. The activists protest seed dependence and unknown risks, and farmers gain a hardier crop and a tie to the seed company that owns it.

right.label: Say no to the special seeds.
right.tooltip: I will not bet the food supply on an untested gene.
right.reveal: You decline to approve it, citing precaution. The pesticide-heavy status quo persists and yields stay flat, but the food supply is not staked on a technology the public distrusts and a seed firm controls.

speaker: Coastal Afforestation Chief
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. Planting a wall of trees by the ocean will stop big waves from killing people during storms. But rich guys want that land for shrimp farms.

left.label: Plant the trees to save people from storms.
left.tooltip: A wall of trees stops a wave no concrete can.
left.reveal: You commit land and money to the greenbelt and protect it from encroachment. The shrimp and salt interests resist losing the coast, but a living storm-barrier rises and the fisheries it nurses revive.

right.label: Let the rich guys use the land for shrimp farms.
right.tooltip: The coast earns more in ponds than in trees.
right.reveal: You let the lucrative shrimp and salt keep the coast. The export earnings flow, the greenbelt goes unplanted, and the next cyclone hits a shore stripped of the trees that would have softened it.

speaker: Forest Conservator
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. Powerful people are sneaking into the protected forest and building hotels. You can kick them out, or pretend you didn't see.

left.label: Kick them out and save the forest.
left.tooltip: A reserve forest sold off in pieces is a lung removed.
left.reveal: You evict the encroachers and reset the boundaries, however connected they are. The powerful grabbers rage and litigate, but the reserve forest is clawed back from the resort-builders and planters.

right.label: Pretend you didn't see anything.
right.tooltip: The men moving those pillars are men I rely on.
right.reveal: You leave the encroachments alone. The connected grabbers keep their slices of forest, the boundary pillars stay where they were quietly moved, and the reserve shrinks resort by resort.

speaker: Renewable Energy Adviser
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. You can force big factories to put solar panels on their roofs and over canals to make clean power. The factory bosses will complain about the cost.

left.label: Force the factories to put up solar panels.
left.tooltip: A nation short of land is rich in roofs and water.
left.reveal: You mandate solar on reservoirs, canals, and rooftops. The utilities and industries grumble at the cost and disruption, but clean power grows without consuming farmland, and imports ease.

right.label: Let the factories choose if they want solar panels or not.
right.tooltip: A mandate forces cost on the unwilling.
right.reveal: You leave solar to voluntary uptake. The disruption and cost are avoided, and the rooftops and reservoirs stay bare while the country keeps burning imported fuel it need not.

speaker: Mining Bureau Director
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. The only rock mine in the country loses money because of a bad contract with a foreign company. If you change it, the rich middlemen will be angry.

left.label: Fix the bad contract and make the mine better.
left.tooltip: A mine that loses money on every stone is a contract, not a mine.
left.reveal: You tear up the padded contract and modernise operations. The connected middlemen who skimmed it howl, but the mine moves toward profit and supplies the construction stone now imported at cost.

right.label: Keep the bad contract and buy rocks from other countries.
right.tooltip: The men running that contract are not lightly crossed.
right.reveal: You leave the loss-making contract intact and import the shortfall. The middlemen stay happy and the mine stays a drain, and the construction boom keeps paying to import stone you have under your own ground.

speaker: Fertilizer Supply Director
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. Farmers need plant food from other countries. You can sign a long, expensive contract to be safe, or just buy it when you need it and hope the price is low.

left.label: Sign the long, expensive contract to be safe.
left.tooltip: A harvest hostage to a world price is a famine on a futures chart.
left.reveal: You sign long-term phosphate and potash contracts and build buffer stocks. The cost and commitment are real, but the farmers are shielded from the next world price spike that would have wrecked a season.

right.label: Buy it cheap right now and risk higher prices later.
right.tooltip: Why pay to hedge a price that might fall?
right.reveal: You keep buying fertilizer feedstock cheaply on the spot market. The savings are real in calm years, and the next price spike passes straight through to the farmers and the harvest.

speaker: Energy Access Director
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. You can give village moms special new stoves that don't make deadly smoke. But they cost money and people are used to their old smoky fires.

left.label: Give out the special new stoves.
left.tooltip: A better stove is a longer life and a standing tree.
left.reveal: You subsidise and distribute clean cookstoves across the villages. The habit is slow to change and the cost real, but women's lungs and the forests both get relief, fire by fire.

right.label: Leave the old smoky fires alone.
right.tooltip: A stove programme is a small return for a real cost.
right.reveal: You skip the cookstove push. The budget is spared, and rural women keep cooking over smoky open fires, their lungs and the surrounding forests paying the quiet price.

speaker: Power Cell Director
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. The government pays crazy high prices for power from factories owned by your friends. You can force them to lower the prices, but they will be mad.

left.label: Force your friends to lower the prices.
left.tooltip: A tariff from a fatter year is a wound in a leaner one.
left.reveal: You force the IPPs to renegotiate toward market rates. The party-linked producers rage and threaten arbitration, but the utility stops bleeding dollars on power priced for a richer decade.

right.label: Keep paying your friends the crazy high prices.
right.tooltip: My donors' contracts are not lightly reopened.
right.reveal: You honour the old terms to keep the donor-producers content. They stay loyal and rich, and the utility keeps paying dollar-indexed tariffs that drain it and, through it, every consumer.

speaker: Water Supply Director
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. The city is drinking all the underground water. You can build a giant system to clean river water instead, but it costs a lot and the rivers are gross.

left.label: Build the giant system to clean river water.
left.tooltip: A city drinking its own foundations away is doomed.
left.reveal: You commit to treated surface water and the river-cleaning it requires. The capital is vast and the build long, but the aquifers steady and the cities gain a sustainable source of drink.

right.label: Keep drinking the underground water.
right.tooltip: Surface water is a budget for the next decade, not this one.
right.reveal: You keep the cities on cheap, fast groundwater. The money is saved now, and the aquifers keep falling and the land keeps sinking toward the day the wells run dry or the ground gives way.

speaker: Livestock Development Director
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. You buy almost all your milk powder from other countries. You can spend a lot of money to help your own farmers make milk, or just keep buying it cheap from away.

left.label: Spend money to help your own farmers make milk.
left.tooltip: A nation that imports its milk has surrendered its breakfast.
left.reveal: You invest in feed, breeds, chilling, and markets for smallholder dairy. The importers fight and the build is slow, but domestic milk grows and a vast cohort of small farmers gains a living.

right.label: Keep buying cheap milk powder from other countries.
right.tooltip: Cheap powder feeds children the budget cannot.
right.reveal: You keep relying on imported powder. Milk stays cheap on the shelf, and the smallholder dairy farmers keep struggling against a foreign product the state will not help them beat.

speaker: Water Development Officer
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. The old village ponds are full of mud. You can pay people to dig them out so fish can live there again, but local bosses might steal some of the money.

left.label: Pay to dig out the old ponds.
left.tooltip: A cleared canal waters a hundred fields.
left.reveal: You launch the mass re-excavation, creating rural jobs and restoring water bodies. Some funds leak to local graft, but irrigation, fisheries, and flood-buffering revive across the countryside.

right.label: Keep it small and safe so nobody steals money.
right.tooltip: A vast labour scheme is a vast skimming opportunity.
right.reveal: You run only a modest, closely-audited version. The graft is contained, and most silted ponds and canals stay choked, their irrigation and flood-buffering lost to the countryside.

speaker: Agriculture Research Director
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. A few companies sell seeds that farmers have to buy brand new every year. You can help farmers save their own seeds, but the seed companies will be angry.

left.label: Help farmers save their own seeds.
left.tooltip: A farmer who cannot save his seed is a tenant of a corporation.
left.reveal: You fund public seed banks and farmer-saved varieties alongside the hybrids. The seed companies lobby furiously, but farmers keep an alternative to buying their seed anew every single season.

right.label: Let the companies keep selling new seeds every year.
right.tooltip: The hybrids yield more; the market knows best.
right.reveal: You leave the seed market to the companies. Yields from the hybrids are high, and the farmers grow ever more dependent on buying patented seed each season from a shrinking handful of firms.

speaker: Petroleum Refining Director
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. The country's lone, ancient oil refinery meets a fraction of fuel demand, so most fuel is imported already-refined at a premium. Build a costly second refinery for self-reliance, or keep importing refined product and skip the capital?

left.label: Build the second refinery.
left.tooltip: A nation that cannot refine its own crude pays twice.
left.reveal: You commit to a modern second refinery. The capital is enormous and the build long, but the country gains the capacity to refine its own and stops paying the premium on imported product.

right.label: Keep importing refined fuel.
right.tooltip: A refinery is a fortune for a margin I can simply pay.
right.reveal: You keep importing refined fuel and skip the capital. The money stays free for other needs, and the country stays dependent on foreign refineries and the premium they charge.

speaker: Water Resources Officer
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. As aquifers fall, a programme of managed recharge — capturing monsoon rain to refill the groundwater — could slow the decline. The officer wants mandatory rainwater harvesting and recharge structures; it is cheap per site but vast in scale and easy to neglect.

left.label: Mandate recharge and rainwater harvesting.
left.tooltip: Water taken from the ground must be paid back to it.
left.reveal: You mandate harvesting and recharge at scale. Enforcement is a grind across countless sites, but the monsoon's water starts refilling the aquifers instead of running wasted to the sea.

right.label: Leave water use to draw down freely.
right.tooltip: A mandate on every roof and field is unenforceable.
right.reveal: You skip the recharge mandate as impractical. The administrative burden is avoided, and the aquifers keep falling as the monsoon runs off unwasted-into-the-sea and the wells sink deeper.

speaker: Agriculture Policy Director
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. The obsession with thirsty rice drains the aquifers and leaves diets and incomes undiversified. The director wants incentives to shift toward less-thirsty, higher-value crops — pulses, oilseeds, vegetables — against the grain of habit and rice-first policy.

left.label: Incentivise crop diversification.
left.tooltip: A country that grows only rice mines its own water.
left.reveal: You incentivise the shift to less-thirsty, higher-value crops. Farmer habit and the rice-first policy resist, but diets diversify, incomes rise, and the aquifer-draining rice monoculture loosens its grip.

right.label: Keep the rice-first policy.
right.tooltip: Rice is food security; do not gamble the staple.
right.reveal: You keep prioritising rice. Food security feels assured and farmers stay on familiar ground, and the thirsty monoculture keeps draining the aquifers and narrowing the nation's diet and incomes.

speaker: Marine Conservation Officer
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. The coast's sea-turtle nesting beaches are being lost to lights, nets, and development. A protected hatchery-and-beach programme would save them and draw eco-tourism, at the cost of restricting the fishing and building the coastal economy leans on.

left.label: Protect the nesting beaches and hatcheries.
left.tooltip: A coast that loses its turtles loses a wonder and a draw.
left.reveal: You ring off nesting beaches, fund hatcheries, and curb the lights and nets. The coastal fishers and developers chafe at the limits, but the turtles return and eco-tourism follows.

right.label: Leave the coast to fishing and building.
right.tooltip: A turtle is a poor return on prime coast.
right.reveal: You leave the beaches to development and nets. The coastal economy keeps its ground, and the turtle populations dwindle toward local extinction under the lights and the trawls.

speaker: Grid Modernisation Director
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. Solar and wind are useless after dark and in calm without storage, and the grid cannot absorb them. Invest in a costly grid-scale battery programme to make renewables dispatchable, or keep leaning on fossil plants for reliable power?

left.label: Invest in grid-scale battery storage.
left.tooltip: Sunshine saved for the evening is sunshine that counts.
left.reveal: You fund battery storage and the grid can finally absorb and time-shift renewables. The capital is steep, but solar and wind become reliable power instead of daytime novelties, and fossil dependence eases.

right.label: Keep leaning on fossil baseload.
right.tooltip: Batteries are a fortune for power I already have.
right.reveal: You skip the storage and keep the fossil plants as backbone. The money is saved, and the renewables you build stay a fair-weather supplement the grid cannot truly rely on.

speaker: Gas Distribution Chief
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. A fifth of the piped gas vanishes through illegal connections, tampered meters, and bribed staff, much of it serving captive industrial and elite users. Mount a hard anti-theft drive that hits connected beneficiaries, or keep absorbing the loss?

left.label: Crack down on the gas theft.
left.tooltip: Gas stolen by the connected is paid for by the metered.
left.reveal: You hunt the illegal connections and tampered meters and purge the complicit staff. The connected thieves and their patrons rage, but the system loss falls and the honest stop subsidising the powerful.

right.label: Absorb the system loss.
right.tooltip: A theft drive against the connected is a war I need not start.
right.reveal: You leave the gas theft alone. The connected keep their illegal taps and tampered meters, and the vanishing fifth of the gas is quietly billed, through tariffs, to those who actually pay.

speaker: Forest Department Chief
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. A social-forestry scheme would let poor villagers grow and harvest trees on degraded state land — income for them, green cover for the country — but done carelessly it becomes a backdoor to clear-felling and grabbing the very forest it should grow. How do you design it?

left.label: Run a genuine community social-forestry scheme.
left.tooltip: A forest the poor profit from is a forest the poor defend.
left.reveal: You design a real benefit-sharing scheme on degraded land with safeguards against grabbing. The poor gain income and the country green cover, and the forest gains guardians instead of poachers.

right.label: Let the scheme run loose.
right.tooltip: Trees planted are trees planted, however it is done.
right.reveal: You roll it out without tight safeguards. The headline planting numbers rise, and the scheme becomes a backdoor for clear-felling and grabbing the forest under cover of growing it.

speaker: Renewable Research Director
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. The strong tidal range of the estuaries could, in theory, drive tidal power — a clean, novel source. A pilot would be a costly, unproven gamble on innovation; the safe path is to stick with proven solar and wind and let others pioneer the tides.

left.label: Fund the tidal-energy pilot.
left.tooltip: A nation of rivers should learn to harvest its tides.
left.reveal: You fund the tidal pilot. It is a gamble on unproven technology, and if it works the estuaries gain a clean, novel power source the country could pioneer for the region.

right.label: Stick with proven solar and wind.
right.tooltip: Let richer nations gamble on the tides.
right.reveal: You skip the tidal gamble for proven renewables. The capital is spared the risk, and the chance to pioneer a novel clean source suited to your estuaries passes to someone else.

speaker: Agriculture Policy Director
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. The country imports almost all its cooking oil, draining dollars and leaving prices hostage to world markets. A push for domestic mustard, sunflower, and soy oilseed would build self-reliance — slow, and competing for the scarce land that grows food grains.

left.label: Drive domestic oilseed self-reliance.
left.tooltip: A kitchen run on imported oil is a kitchen run from abroad.
left.reveal: You incentivise oilseed cultivation and crushing. It competes for scarce land and the payoff is slow, but the cooking-oil import bill begins to fall and prices steady against world shocks.

right.label: Keep importing cheap cooking oil.
right.tooltip: Imported oil is cheap and the land is needed for rice.
right.reveal: You keep relying on imported edible oil. The land stays in grain and the oil stays cheap on the shelf, and every world price spike passes straight into the nation's kitchens.

speaker: Sugar Corporation Chief
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. The state sugar mills lose money every year on obsolete machinery and graft, but tens of thousands of cane farmers and mill workers depend on them. Shut or privatise the worst to stem the losses, or pour in another bailout and modernisation gamble?

left.label: Shut or privatise the loss-making mills.
left.tooltip: A mill that only crushes losses crushes nothing.
left.reveal: You close or sell the worst mills and stem the bleeding. The freed budget is real, and the cane farmers and mill towns built around the mills are left stranded and furious.

right.label: Bail out and modernise the mills.
right.tooltip: The cane belt has nowhere else to turn.
right.reveal: You fund a modernisation gamble to save the mills and the cane belt. The cost is heavy and the odds uncertain, but the farmers and workers keep their livelihood and a chance at a viable industry.

speaker: Agriculture Marketing Director
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. A third of the fruit and vegetables rot between farm and market for want of storage, cold chain, and roads — a staggering waste that beggars farmers and inflates prices. The director wants a costly cold-chain build; the capital is large and the gain diffuse.

left.label: Build the cold chain and storage.
left.tooltip: A harvest that rots on the road feeds no one and ruins the grower.
left.reveal: You invest in cold storage, packhouses, and farm-to-market links. The capital is heavy, but the rotting waste falls sharply, farmers earn more, and consumers pay less for what no longer spoils.

right.label: Leave the supply chain as it is.
right.tooltip: A cold chain is a fortune for a problem the market lives with.
right.reveal: You leave the produce to rot as it always has. The capital is spared, and a third of the harvest keeps spoiling between farm and plate, beggaring farmers and inflating prices.

speaker: Industries Resource Officer
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. The coal plants pile up fly-ash as toxic waste, while the brick kilns devour the country's topsoil. Mandating fly-ash bricks would solve both — using the waste, sparing the soil — but it disrupts the entrenched clay-brick industry and its workers.

left.label: Mandate fly-ash brick production.
left.tooltip: A waste that builds is better than a soil that burns.
left.reveal: You mandate fly-ash bricks. The clay-kiln industry resists and some workers are displaced, but the toxic ash finds a use, the topsoil is spared, and the smoggy clay kilns lose their grip.

right.label: Leave the clay-brick industry alone.
right.tooltip: A million kiln workers outweigh a pile of ash.
right.reveal: You leave the clay kilns be. The workers keep their jobs, and the fly-ash keeps piling up toxic while the kilns keep burning the country's topsoil into bricks.

speaker: Marine Resources Director
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. Declaring a large marine protected area in the bay would let the overfished stocks recover and safeguard biodiversity — a long-term gift to the fishery — but it locks both trawlers and artisanal fishers out of rich grounds in the short term.

left.label: Declare the marine protected area.
left.tooltip: A sea given rest is a sea that feeds again.
left.reveal: You ring off the protected area and the stocks within it rebound, spilling fish into the surrounding waters over time. The trawlers and fishers locked out protest the short-term loss bitterly.

right.label: Keep the grounds open to fishing.
right.tooltip: A fisher kept off the water today does not eat today.
right.reveal: You leave the rich grounds open. The fishers keep their catch this season, and the overfished stocks slide further toward collapse, taking the fishery's future down with them.

speaker: Grid Expansion Director
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. New power plants sit idle for want of transmission lines, and the lines are stalled by land disputes and farmers refusing the towers across their fields. Force the right-of-way through with compulsory acquisition, or negotiate slow and fair?

left.label: Force the right-of-way through.
left.tooltip: A plant that cannot send its power is a monument.
left.reveal: You push the lines through with compulsory acquisition. The towers rise fast and the stranded plants finally deliver, and the dispossessed farmers, paid little and asked less, nurse a lasting grievance.

right.label: Negotiate fair, slow right-of-way.
right.tooltip: A tower on a man's field should be a deal, not a decree.
right.reveal: You negotiate fair compensation and routing with the farmers. The lines come slowly and the plants stay idle longer, but the right-of-way is won by consent rather than coercion.

speaker: Plantation Affairs Officer
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. Tea companies want to expand their estates into adjoining reserve forest and indigenous land — more export tea, at the cost of felled forest and displaced hill communities. Grant the leases, or protect the forest and the people on it?

left.label: Grant the tea-estate expansion.
left.tooltip: Tea is dollars; forest is just trees.
left.reveal: You grant the leases and the estates spread into the forest. Export tea grows, and felled forest and displaced hill communities pay for it, and another grievance hardens in the hills.

right.label: Protect the forest and the communities.
right.tooltip: An estate built on a felled forest and a displaced people is a poor brew.
right.reveal: You refuse the expansion into forest and indigenous land. The tea companies lose their growth and grumble, but the forest stands and the hill communities keep the ground beneath them.

speaker: Irrigation Technology Director
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. Replacing the diesel and grid pumps that irrigate the fields with solar ones would slash the fuel bill and emissions and free farmers from fuel-price shocks — a large upfront cost, against the cheap, familiar diesel status quo.

left.label: Drive the solar-irrigation rollout.
left.tooltip: A pump that runs on sunlight runs on nothing dear.
left.reveal: You subsidise solar irrigation pumps across the farmland. The upfront cost is large, but farmers escape diesel-price shocks, the fuel bill and emissions fall, and the fields water themselves on sunshine.

right.label: Stick with diesel and grid pumps.
right.tooltip: A solar pump is a fortune against a jerrycan of diesel.
right.reveal: You leave irrigation on diesel and grid power. The upfront cost is dodged, and the farmers stay hostage to fuel prices while the pumps keep burning diesel and draining the subsidy.

speaker: Coastal Fisheries Officer
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. The dried-fish trade — a coastal staple and growing export — is rife with illegal pesticide use to keep flies off the racks, poisoning consumers and threatening the export market. The officer wants enforcement and clean drying tech; the traders resist the cost.

left.label: Enforce clean drying, ban the pesticides.
left.tooltip: A staple cured in poison is a slow harm to millions.
left.reveal: You ban the pesticides and fund clean drying technology. The traders resist the cost, but the dried fish on the nation's plates and in the export crates stops carrying its hidden poison.

right.label: Leave the dried-fish trade alone.
right.tooltip: The trade is cheap and the demand is high.
right.reveal: You leave the drying racks and their pesticides be. The trade stays cheap and busy, and the dried fish keeps reaching plates and export markets laced with the chemicals that keep the flies off.

speaker: Geological Survey Director
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. A thorough national mineral survey could reveal coal, gas, hard rock, and rare minerals worth fortunes — knowledge that is power, and a magnet for foreign extractors and the conflicts that follow discovery. Survey aggressively, or tread lightly?

left.label: Survey aggressively and map the wealth.
left.tooltip: A nation ignorant of its own ground is poorer for it.
left.reveal: You fund an aggressive survey and the map of the country's mineral wealth fills in. Knowledge is gained, and so is the attention of foreign extractors and the disputes that ride on every discovery.

right.label: Survey cautiously and slowly.
right.tooltip: A discovery is a curse as often as a blessing.
right.reveal: You proceed with a slow, cautious survey. You forgo the rush of knowledge and investment, and avoid lighting the fuse of extraction conflicts and foreign appetite that a full mineral map would.

speaker: Coastal Afforestation Officer
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. Planting forests on the new coastal chars would stabilise the silt islands, buffer storms, and eventually create land for the landless — slow, unglamorous work, easily neglected against the rush to hand the chars to shrimp farms and grabbers.

left.label: Fund char afforestation and stabilisation.
left.tooltip: A planted char becomes solid ground for the landless.
left.reveal: You fund the slow work of foresting and stabilising the chars. The shrimp interests and grabbers eye the land hungrily, but the islands solidify, buffer the storms, and become future homes for the landless.

right.label: Hand the chars to shrimp and developers.
right.tooltip: The chars earn more in ponds than in saplings.
right.reveal: You let the chars go to shrimp farms and grabbers. The export earnings flow and the powerful are pleased, and the unstabilised islands stay storm-prone and the landless landless.

speaker: Land Reform Commissioner
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. Land-ceiling laws meant to cap how much one family can own are flouted wholesale through benami holdings, while millions are landless. The commissioner wants real enforcement and redistribution of the surplus; the great landholders, many connected, will fight hard.

left.label: Enforce the land ceiling and redistribute.
left.tooltip: Land hoarded past the law is land owed to the landless.
left.reveal: You enforce the ceiling, pierce the benami holdings, and redistribute surplus land. The great landholders rage and pull every string, but millions of the landless gain ground of their own.

right.label: Leave the land ceiling unenforced.
right.tooltip: The great landholders are the spine of rural power.
right.reveal: You leave the ceiling a dead letter. The connected landlords keep their vast holdings behind false names, and the landless millions stay landless, tenants and labourers on ground the law says they should share.

speaker: Farm Mechanisation Director
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. Mechanising the farms — harvesters, tillers, transplanters — would raise yields and ease the brutal labour as workers leave for the cities. But it would also throw landless farm labourers, who have nothing else, out of their only work. How hard do you push?

left.label: Push mechanisation hard.
left.tooltip: A farm that cannot find hands must find machines.
left.reveal: You subsidise farm machinery widely. Yields rise and the labour eases as workers drift cityward, and the landless labourers who relied on harvest wages find the machines have taken their last work.

right.label: Mechanise gently, protect farm labour.
right.tooltip: A machine that idles the landless feeds the slum, not the field.
right.reveal: You phase mechanisation in slowly with retraining for displaced labourers. Yields rise more modestly, but the landless are not thrown off the land all at once into the cities' slums.

speaker: Industrial Waste Officer
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. Used engine oil and scrap tyres are dumped or burned in toxic backyard operations that poison soil and air. A formal recycling and pyrolysis industry could turn the waste to value cleanly — at a cost the informal burners will resist.

left.label: Build formal oil and tyre recycling.
left.tooltip: A tyre burned in a yard is a poison breathed in a slum.
left.reveal: You license clean recycling and pyrolysis and crack down on backyard burning. The informal operators resist losing their cheap trade, but the toxic dumping and burning recede and the waste finds clean value.

right.label: Leave waste-oil and tyres to the informal trade.
right.tooltip: The backyard burners harm no one the budget answers for.
right.reveal: You leave the backyard burning and dumping be. The cheap informal trade carries on, and the used oil and burning tyres keep poisoning the soil, the air, and the slums around them.

speaker: Energy Regulator
prompt: It comes back sharpened by every season you survived. A generous feed-in tariff guaranteeing a good price for renewable power fed to the grid would unleash private solar and wind investment — at a cost locked in for years that the utility, and ultimately the consumer, must bear. How generous do you set it?

left.label: Set a generous feed-in tariff.
left.tooltip: A guaranteed price is the spark that lights the investment.
left.reveal: You set an attractive feed-in tariff and private renewable investment surges. Clean capacity grows fast, and the above-market price is locked in for years, a cost the utility and consumers carry.

right.label: Set a lean, market-linked tariff.
right.tooltip: A subsidy locked in for years is a debt locked in for years.
right.reveal: You keep the tariff lean and market-linked. The renewable investment comes slower and thinner, but the grid and consumers are not saddled with years of above-market payments.

speaker: Maritime Resources Envoy
prompt: A later year of power, the old crisis now a true test of it. With the maritime boundary settled, you can press an extended continental-shelf claim to the seabed beyond — a potential bonanza of gas and minerals, and a costly, years-long legal and survey effort that may antagonise neighbours with overlapping claims.

left.label: Press the extended shelf claim.
left.tooltip: The seabed beyond the line may hold the next bonanza.
left.reveal: You mount the costly claim to the extended shelf. The survey and legal effort are long, and if it succeeds the country gains rights to vast seabed gas and minerals — and the neighbours' overlapping resentment.

right.label: Settle for the established boundary.
right.tooltip: A bird of gas in hand beats a seabed in court.
right.reveal: You content yourself with the settled waters. You forgo a potential seabed bonanza and the costly fight for it, and keep the maritime peace with your neighbours unruffled.

speaker: Forest Tourism Officer
prompt: The unresolved returns, and this time it will not be deferred. A grand safari park and eco-resort carved out of a forest reserve would draw tourists and revenue — and fragment the habitat, cage wildlife for show, and hand prime forest to resort developers. Build the attraction, or protect the reserve intact?

left.label: Build the safari park and eco-resort.
left.tooltip: A forest that pays for itself survives.
left.reveal: You greenlight the safari park and resort. Tourist revenue flows and the project dazzles, and the reserve is fragmented, its wildlife caged for show and its prime tracts handed to developers.

right.label: Protect the reserve intact.
right.tooltip: A caged forest is a zoo, not a wild.
right.reveal: You refuse to carve up the reserve for an attraction. The tourist revenue is forgone and the developers disappointed, but the habitat stays whole and its wildlife wild.

speaker: Biofuel Policy Officer
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. Molasses from the sugar mills could be turned into ethanol to blend with petrol, cutting fuel imports and using a waste stream — or that same feedstock could feed the food and drink industries. Mandate biofuel blending?

left.label: Mandate ethanol blending.
left.tooltip: A waste turned to fuel is a dollar kept home.
left.reveal: You mandate ethanol blending from molasses. The fuel-import bill eases and a waste stream finds value, and the food and drink industries that wanted the same molasses pay more for it.

right.label: Keep molasses for food and drink.
right.tooltip: I will not turn the nation's sugar into the nation's petrol.
right.reveal: You decline the blending mandate. The molasses stays with the food industries, and the chance to cut fuel imports with a domestic biofuel goes untaken.

speaker: Power Planning Director
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. With little room for big dams, the engineers propose a pumped-storage scheme in the hills — a giant battery using water and gravity to store surplus power. It is costly, geologically risky, and would flood a hill valley, displacing its communities.

left.label: Build the pumped-storage scheme.
left.tooltip: A mountain battery is worth a flooded valley.
left.reveal: You build the pumped-storage project. The grid gains vast storage and renewables become dispatchable, and a hill valley is flooded and its communities displaced for the reservoir.

right.label: Drop the pumped-storage plan.
right.tooltip: I will not drown a valley to store electrons.
right.reveal: You cancel the scheme. The grid loses a great store of power and renewables stay harder to balance, but the hill valley and the communities in it are spared the flood.

speaker: Food Fortification Officer
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. Iodine deficiency still stunts minds in the regions where un-iodised salt slips through, and enforcement of universal salt iodisation is lax. The officer wants a hard enforcement drive; the small, scattered salt producers resist the cost and oversight.

left.label: Enforce universal salt iodisation.
left.tooltip: A pinch of iodine is a child's whole future.
left.reveal: You enforce iodisation hard and monitor the supply. The small producers resist the cost, but iodine deficiency and the stunting it causes recede from the regions that fell through the gaps.

right.label: Leave iodisation lightly enforced.
right.tooltip: The salt trade is too scattered to police.
right.reveal: You leave enforcement lax. The administrative burden is avoided, and un-iodised salt keeps slipping through to the regions where it quietly goes on stunting children's minds.

speaker: Food Storage Director
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. The country's grain reserve rots in damp, rat-infested warehouses, losing a fortune and leaving the buffer unreliable in a crisis. The director wants modern steel silos; the capital is large and the warehouse rackets that profit from the chaos resist.

left.label: Build modern grain silos.
left.tooltip: A reserve that rots is no reserve at all.
left.reveal: You build modern silos and the grain reserve becomes real and reliable. The capital is steep and the warehouse rackets lose their cosy losses, but the food buffer can finally be trusted in a crisis.

right.label: Keep the old warehouses.
right.tooltip: Silos are a fortune for grain that mostly keeps.
right.reveal: You keep the damp warehouses and their rackets. The capital is spared, the grain keeps rotting and the rats keep feeding, and the reserve stays a number on paper that a real crisis would expose.

speaker: Livestock Health Director
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. The booming poultry industry — cheap protein for millions — is a tinderbox for bird flu, with poor biosecurity and culling-compensation too stingy to stop farmers hiding outbreaks. The director wants real biosecurity and fair compensation; it is costly.

left.label: Fund biosecurity and fair culling pay.
left.tooltip: A hidden outbreak today is a pandemic tomorrow.
left.reveal: You fund biosecurity standards and fair compensation so farmers report outbreaks instead of hiding them. The cost is real, but bird flu is contained before it can leap to a wider catastrophe.

right.label: Leave poultry biosecurity thin.
right.tooltip: The industry has weathered flu before.
right.reveal: You leave biosecurity weak and compensation stingy. The cheap chicken keeps coming, and farmers keep hiding outbreaks until one slips its cage into a crisis the thin defences cannot hold.

speaker: Land Reform Officer
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. Millions of sharecroppers till others' land for a punishing share of the crop, with no security and no recourse when a landlord evicts them at whim. The officer wants enforceable tenancy rights and a fairer share; the landlord class resists fiercely.

left.label: Enforce tenancy rights and a fair share.
left.tooltip: A farmer who can be evicted at whim plants in fear.
left.reveal: You legislate secure tenancy and a fairer crop-share, and enforce it. The landlord class fights bitterly through every local lever, but the sharecroppers gain security and a fairer return on their toil.

right.label: Leave the tenancy system as it is.
right.tooltip: The landlords are the rural order I depend on.
right.reveal: You leave the sharecroppers to the landlords' mercy. The rural power structure stays content, and millions keep tilling on a punishing share, one whim away from eviction.

speaker: Energy Efficiency Director
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. The cheapest power is the power not used, and mandatory energy-efficiency standards for buildings, motors, and appliances would cut demand more cheaply than any new plant. The director wants hard standards; manufacturers and developers resist the cost.

left.label: Mandate hard efficiency standards.
left.tooltip: A kilowatt saved is a kilowatt I need not build.
left.reveal: You mandate efficiency standards across buildings, motors, and appliances. Manufacturers and developers grumble at the cost, but demand growth slows, the grid breathes, and you avoid building plants you would otherwise have needed.

right.label: Leave efficiency to the market.
right.tooltip: A mandate is a cost forced on the builder and the buyer.
right.reveal: You leave efficiency voluntary. The builders and manufacturers keep their cheap, wasteful ways, and demand climbs unchecked, forcing you to build power you could have simply saved.

speaker: Marine Fisheries Director
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. Your fishers work only the crowded coastal waters while the rich deep-sea zone of your own maritime territory is fished by licensed foreign trawlers. The director wants a national deep-sea fleet to claim that catch; it is costly and the fishers lack the vessels and skills.

left.label: Build a national deep-sea fleet.
left.tooltip: My own deep waters should feed my own fishers.
left.reveal: You invest in deep-sea vessels, training, and landing infrastructure. The cost is heavy and the skills slow to build, but the catch from your own deep waters starts flowing to your own fishers instead of foreign trawlers.

right.label: Keep licensing the foreign trawlers.
right.tooltip: The fees are sure; a fleet is a gamble.
right.reveal: You keep taking the foreign trawler licence fees. The revenue is easy and certain, and the wealth of your own deep waters keeps sailing away in foreign holds while your fishers crowd the coast.

speaker: Agriculture Export Director
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. The country's mangoes, jackfruit, and vegetables could earn real export money, but they rot for want of cold chain, fail foreign pesticide tests, and lack branding. Invest in a costly push for export-grade horticulture, or keep them a domestic crop?

left.label: Invest in export-grade horticulture.
left.tooltip: A mango that meets the world's bar earns the world's price.
left.reveal: You fund cold chain, residue control, and branding for fruit and vegetable exports. The investment is real, but a new export stream and better farmer incomes grow from crops that once just rotted or sold cheap.

right.label: Keep horticulture a domestic crop.
right.tooltip: Feeding my own market is achievement enough.
right.reveal: You leave the fruit and vegetables to the domestic market. The export earnings are forgone, and the surplus keeps rotting in gluts that crash prices and beggar the growers each season.

speaker: Cottage Resources Officer
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. Bamboo and cane sustain vast craft and construction livelihoods and could anchor a sustainable green industry — but the resource is being stripped bare and the artisans are sliding into poverty. Build a managed-cultivation and value-chain push, or let it dwindle?

left.label: Build a sustainable bamboo value chain.
left.tooltip: A fast-growing reed is a green industry waiting to be grown.
left.reveal: You fund managed bamboo cultivation, processing, and the artisans' value chain. A renewable green industry takes root, livelihoods stabilise, and the wild stands are spared the strip-harvesting.

right.label: Let the bamboo trade dwindle.
right.tooltip: A reed and a basket are not a development strategy.
right.reveal: You leave the bamboo trade to its decline. The investment is spared, and the wild stands keep being stripped while the artisans slide deeper into poverty and the craft fades.

speaker: Aquaculture Director
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. Disease keeps wiping out the shrimp farms — a major export — because the hatcheries sell weak, virus-laden post-larvae and farmers lack any quality control. The director wants certified disease-free hatcheries and training; the cheap-PL hatchery trade resists.

left.label: Mandate certified disease-free hatcheries.
left.tooltip: A pond stocked with sick larvae is a harvest already lost.
left.reveal: You mandate certified, disease-free post-larvae and train farmers in biosecurity. The cheap-PL hatcheries fight the cost, but the devastating disease wipeouts ebb and the shrimp export stabilises.

right.label: Leave the hatchery trade unregulated.
right.tooltip: The cheap larvae keep the ponds stocked.
right.reveal: You leave the hatcheries to sell their cheap, weak larvae. The ponds stay cheaply stocked, and disease keeps sweeping the farms, wiping out harvests and the export earnings with them.

speaker: Urban Energy Officer
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. Air-conditioning is exploding with the heat and the middle class, spiking peak demand and grid strain. A district-cooling and efficiency push could blunt it; the developers and the AC-importing lobby prefer the cheap, wasteful status quo.

left.label: Push district cooling and AC standards.
left.tooltip: A city cooled efficiently is a grid not buckled.
left.reveal: You promote district cooling and strict AC efficiency standards. The developers and importers resist, but the peak demand growth slows, sparing the grid the strain of cooling a warming, richer city.

right.label: Let cooling demand run.
right.tooltip: A growing middle class has earned its cool air.
right.reveal: You leave cooling to grow unchecked. The middle class enjoys its air-conditioning, peak demand spikes, and the grid strains and the bills climb to chill a city you could have cooled more cheaply.

speaker: Water Development Engineer
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. A vast river-training scheme could tame the great river's wandering, stop the erosion swallowing districts, and reclaim land — a mega-project of guide bunds and revetments, hugely costly, technically fraught, and a magnet for contractor padding.

left.label: Launch the river-training mega-project.
left.tooltip: A river that wanders unchecked eats a district a year.
left.reveal: You launch the guide-bund and revetment scheme. The cost is colossal and the contractors pad it, but if the engineering holds, the river is pinned, the erosion slows, and land is reclaimed.

right.label: Stick with local erosion protection.
right.tooltip: A grand scheme to tame a great river is a fortune gambled on hydraulics.
right.reveal: You fund only targeted local bank protection. The grand reclamation is forgone, the river keeps wandering and eating its banks, but you avoid betting a fortune on a fraught mega-project.

speaker: Food Standards Director
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. A credible safe-food and organic certification scheme would let farmers earn premiums and reassure consumers poisoned by adulteration — a costly system to build and police, easily faked into a meaningless sticker if done on the cheap.

left.label: Build a rigorous certification scheme.
left.tooltip: A label that means nothing is worse than no label.
left.reveal: You build a properly policed certification system. The cost and enforcement are real, but the certified food earns genuine premiums, consumers gain trust, and the export door opens to clean produce.

right.label: Run a cheap, lightly-policed scheme.
right.tooltip: A sticker is cheaper than a system.
right.reveal: You roll out a low-cost certification with weak policing. The labels proliferate and quickly become meaningless as fakes flood in, and consumers and export buyers learn to distrust the sticker entirely.

speaker: Utility Modernisation Director
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. Smart meters would end the bribed meter-readers, estimated bills, and theft that plague the utilities — and let you bill accurately and cut losses. They are costly to roll out, and consumers fear higher, harder-to-dodge bills.

left.label: Roll out smart meters widely.
left.tooltip: A meter that cannot be bribed bills the truth.
left.reveal: You fund a wide smart-meter rollout. The capital is heavy and consumers wary of accurate, undodgeable bills, but the meter-reader bribery and theft collapse and the utilities' losses fall sharply.

right.label: Stick with the old meters.
right.tooltip: A meter rollout is a fortune and a fight over every bill.
right.reveal: You keep the old meters and their bribed readers. The capital is spared and consumers undisturbed, and the estimated bills, the theft, and the meter-reader rackets all carry quietly on.

speaker: Biodiversity Resources Officer
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. The country's forests hold medicinal plants and genetic resources that foreign firms quietly harvest and patent — biopiracy of a heritage worth fortunes. The officer wants a benefit-sharing and protection regime; it is complex and could deter research investment too.

left.label: Build a benefit-sharing protection regime.
left.tooltip: A heritage patented abroad is a heritage stolen.
left.reveal: You establish access rules and benefit-sharing for the country's genetic resources. The bureaucracy is complex, but biopiracy is curbed and the communities and the nation share in the value of their own heritage.

right.label: Leave the genetic resources open.
right.tooltip: Rules on every leaf will scare off the researchers too.
right.reveal: You leave access unregulated to keep research investment flowing. The foreign firms keep harvesting and patenting freely, and a heritage worth fortunes drains abroad uncompensated.

speaker: Inland Fisheries Director
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. The open-water fisheries — the beels and floodplains feeding millions — are being plundered by leaseholders and gear that wipe out the stocks. The director wants community co-management handing the waters to the fishers; the connected leaseholders resist.

left.label: Hand the waters to community co-management.
left.tooltip: A fishery the fishers govern is a fishery that lasts.
left.reveal: You transfer the open waters to community co-management with rules the fishers set and keep. The connected leaseholders lose their plunder and rage, but the stocks recover and the fishing communities thrive.

right.label: Keep the leasehold system.
right.tooltip: The leaseholders pay their fees and back the party.
right.reveal: You keep the waters under the connected leaseholders. The fees and the loyalty flow, and the leaseholders keep plundering the beels with destructive gear until the stocks, and the fishers' living, collapse.

speaker: Renewable Energy Officer
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. Rural cattle dung and farm waste could fuel village-scale biogas plants — clean cooking gas and fertiliser slurry from waste — cutting woodsmoke and firewood demand. The officer wants a national biogas push; uptake has always stalled on cost and habit.

left.label: Drive a national biogas programme.
left.tooltip: A cow's waste can light a kitchen and feed a field.
left.reveal: You subsidise and support village biogas plants at scale. Maintenance and habit slow the uptake, but clean cooking gas and fertiliser flow from waste, easing woodsmoke and firewood demand where it takes hold.

right.label: Leave biogas to the enthusiasts.
right.tooltip: A scheme that always stalls is a scheme not worth the subsidy.
